content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

June 26, 1996

Stephen J. Kaufman
Dario J. Frommer
Long & Levit
601 South Figueroa Street, Suite 2500
Los Angeles, California 90017-5704

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-175

Dear Messrs. Kaufman and Frommer:

This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
personal use of campaign funds provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

1. May campaign committee funds be used to pay for or
reimburse the cost of a union's political staff salaries where the
individual staff members engage in work that is both related to
general union business and to administrative functions of the
political committee?

2. Assuming that all or some portion of the union's
political staff salaries may be paid out of committee funds, are
such payments barred under California Government Code section
89513.5 if those individuals have authority to approve the
expenditure of some campaign committee funds?

CONCLUSION

Committee funds may be used for any expenditure, including
staff salaries, reasonably related to a political, legislative or
governmental purpose. Expenditures made by a staff member with
authority to approve committee expenditures must be directly
related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose if the
expenditures confer a personal benefit on the staff member. This

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations
appear at Title 2, Sections 18000-18995 of the California Code of
Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File NO. A-96-175
Page 2


advice is limited to the provisions of the Political Reform Act.
You may want to consult a tax advisor for assistance on federal
and state tax laws governing this area.

FACTS

Local 660 is a labor organization duly chartered by the
Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO. Local 660 has
established a "COPE check off" program whereby members of the
union may voluntarily designate that a portion of their union dues
be deposited into COPE (COMMITTEE ON POLITICAL EDUCATION) accounts
("COPE Funds"). This program enables Local 660 to make
contributions to campaigns for local and state elected offices and
to pass or defeat initiatives or referenda. COPE Funds are used
in electoral, legislative, and initiative campaigns. The COPE
Committee researches and reviews candidates and issues and makes
recommendations to the Board of Directors about who to support and
how.

Local 660's COPE funds are disbursed through two registered
political committees: the Issues and Initiatives Committee and
the Los Angeles County Employees Association Inc. Local 660's
Committee on Political Education recommends which officeholders,
candidates, or initiative or referenda campaigns should receive
contributions, subject to approval by the Local's Board of
Directors.

Local 660 represents approximately 40,000 public sector
employees in Los Angeles County. Because representation of the
employees requires the union to lobby, negotiate with and work
with elected officials who run the county, state government and
various special districts (the employers of the members of the
union's bargaining units), Local 660 maintains a political
department.

For many years, the political department's staff salaries
have been paid from the union's general fund. In March of this
year, the Local 660 Board of Directors proposed a change in that
policy. It was proposed that, effective January 1, 1997, 50
percent of political staff salaries would be paid out of COPE
Funds, and that the amount would increase 5 percent annually until
100 percent of the political staff salaries are paid from COPE
Funds. Benefits would continue to be paid out of the union's
general fund.

Much of the work performed by the union's political
department staff relates to routine union business of representing
the interests of both union and nonunion county employees with
regard to government bodies and agencies which affect them. The
political department staff attend membership meetings and work-
site meetings. They coordinate political action by both union and
nonunion members of the various bargaining units aimed at
influencing contract negotiations, or budgeting decisions
affecting the bargaining units. The political department staff

File No. A-96-175
Page 3


participates in union membership recruitment, and the department
coordinates logistics for union rallies, including obtaining
requisite permits from police, fire, or traffic authorities.

The department staff also represents the union at various
public and political events, and at meetings of the various state
and local labor affiliates and organizations. The staff writes
articles for "The Voice," the union's official newspaper, and the
director of the political department has a weekly radio show on
KPFK-FM. The political department staff also provides political
education to both union and nonunion members concerning issues
surrounding contract negotiations and county budget issues. They
keep track of and analyze legislation, initiatives, directives,
and administrative decisions which affect representation of the
bargaining units, negotiation of contracts, or the general welfare
of the union's membership. The department directs the union's
lobbying of California legislators and the Los Angeles Board of
Supervisors, and special district boards on various issues
relating to the state, county and district budgets, public
employment, and other issues of concern to the union or its
members. The staff attends meetings of the Los Angeles County
Board of Supervisors and often travels to Sacramento to meet with
legislators.

The union may direct the department to recruit volunteers for
non-partisan voter registration drives, absentee ballot drives,
letter writing campaigns and phone banks to influence elected
officials to support or oppose bills or issues of concern to the
union members and represents the union at a variety of community
events, forums, and meetings.

In addition, the political department administers the union's
COPE program. The staff sets up COPE meetings; prepares agendas
for the members; prepares a budget for COPE expenditures; reviews
requests for contributions from candidates, officeholders, and
committees; and makes recommendations thereon to the COPE
Committee. The staff keeps track of COPE expenditures and
maintains necessary records. The staff also recruits and trains
volunteers to work on campaigns that the union is interested in
influencing.

The political department staff has the authority to make some
discretionary expenditures under the budget priorities approved by
the board. These expenditures include supplies, general overhead
costs, reimbursement of staff or members for costs and expenses
directly related to the COPE Committee's purpose, and retention of
professional services related to COPE's purpose.

ANALYSIS

The Act places certain restrictions on the use of campaign
funds. "Campaign funds" include any contributions, cash, cash
equivalents and other assets received or possessed by a committee.
Section (89511(b)(1).) Section 89512.5 governs expenditures by

File No. A-96-175
Page 4


committees that are not controlled by candidates.  Expenditures
made by such a committee are required to be "reasonably related to
a political, legislative, or governmental purpose."  (Section
89512.5(c).)  Section 89512.5(b) further provides that:

     Any expenditure by a committee that confers a
     substantial personal benefit on any individual or
     individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of
     campaign funds held by the committee, shall be directly
     related to a political, legislative, or governmental
     purpose of the committee.

                              (Section 89512.5(b).)

     Thus, for the most part, committee expenditures must be
reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose.  If the expenditure will confer a substantial personal
benefit on the person with authority to approve the expenditure,
it must be directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose.  (Section 89512.5(b).)  Committee funds may
not be used for purposes that are not related to a political,
legislative or governmental purpose, such as general union
operating expenses.  (Pappy Advice Letter, No. A-94-031.)

Directly Related

     As noted above, if the expenditures are made by a person with
authority to approve the expenditures, they must be directly
related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose.  In
response to your specific question about salaries, you may use
committee funds to pay the salary of a person with authority to
approve expenditures if the compensation is directly related to a
political, legislative or governmental purpose.  In the past, we
have found that a variety of expenditures are directly related to
a political, legislative or governmental purpose.

     For instance, legislative monitoring and advocacy have been
found to be directly related to a political, legislative or
governmental purpose.  (Weintraub Advice Letter, No. A-90-210.)
An example of an expenditure conferring a personal benefit
includes use of funds to reimburse expenses of a committee member
who travels as a committee representative to a political
fundraiser.  (Expenditure permitted in Ford Advice Letter, No. A-
95-406.)  The directly related standard is more strict than the
reasonably related standard.  Therefore, if an expenditure is
directly related to a political, legislative or governmental
purpose, it will also be reasonably related.

     You have listed specific activities for which you anticipate
using committee funds.  Activities such as participation in
political events and rallies, coordinating political action aimed
at influencing contract negotiations or budget decisions, voter
registration activities, providing political education to both
union and nonunion members, tracking legislation, initiatives and

File No. A-96-175
Page 5


administrative decisions, lobbying state and local officials,
recruiting campaign volunteers, voter registration activities,
get-out-the-vote activities, phone banks to influence elections or
governmental activities, and administrative costs and tasks for
all of these activities appear to be directly related to
political, legislative or governmental purposes. Committee funds
may be used to pay the costs of these activities, including staff
salaries.

Reasonably Related

     Committee funds may be used to pay for activities that are
reasonably related to a political, legislative or governmental
purpose. Educational seminars for a government employee's union's
members to enhance their ability to perform governmental duties
for the state were found to be reasonably related to a political,
legislative or governmental purpose. (Brown Advice Letter, No. I-
90-412.) Further, we have advised that committee funds used to
raise money to renovate a city's community meeting room was
reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose. (Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-94-095.)

     Conversely, in 1994 we advised that a union political action
committee could not donate its contributions to its district
council for normal operating expenses because the union operating
expenses were not reasonably related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose. (Pappy Advice Letter, supra.)
Similarly, we recently advised that an association's expenditure
of committee funds to join an economic development advisory board
was not reasonably related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. (Waters Advice Letter, No. A-96-149.)

     Committee funds may be used for other activities you
mentioned, such as attending union membership and work site
meetings, membership recruitment, union rallies, attending
meetings of state and local affiliates, writing articles,
participating in a radio show, and representing the union at
community events, forums and meetings only if such activities are
reasonably related to a political, legislative or governmental
purpose. Whether these activities further such purposes is a
factual determination. For example, we do not have enough facts
to determine whether the articles written or the topics discussed
on the radio show are political, legislative or governmental. To
the extent any expenditure is not reasonably related to a
political, legislative or governmental purpose, you may not use
COPE funds to pay for staff time or expenses for such activities.
Further, if the expenditure is reasonably related, but not
directly related to such purposes, the funds may not be used to
confer a personal benefit on anyone with authority to approve
expenditures.

     Finally, you requested advice regarding the type of
bookkeeping procedures necessary to ensure the proper use of
committee funds. We decline to provide specific bookkeeping

File No. A-96-175
Page 6


advice on how to segregate the types of expenditures contemplated.
You may employ any reasonable accounting method supported by
accurate recordkeeping to ensure that committee funds are used
only for permissible purposes.

     If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        By:  Liane Randolph
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LR:ak
